citation: Lapré MA, Palazzolo EM. Does Draft Currency Promote Competitive Balance? An Empirical Investigation of the National Football League 2002–2021. Journal of Sports Economics. 2024;1-23. doi:10.1177/15270025241264238
abstract: This study empirically investigates whether the NFL draft promotes competitive balance by examining whether draft-pick value (draft currency) increases the probability of reaching the playoffs across the 2002–2021 seasons. The authors distinguish between original draft currency (value of picks assigned before any trades) and final draft currency (value of picks used after all trades) using eight different draft-value curves. Logistic regression models with team-strength controls show that original draft currency does not affect playoff probability, but final draft currency (reflecting pick trading) does—with a delay of 2–3 years. Teams that outperformed others in trading draft picks, most notably the New England Patriots, perpetuated competitive imbalance by exploiting outdated pick-value frameworks used by most teams.
tldr: The NFL draft's intent to promote competitive balance fails in practice because what matters is not where you pick but how smartly you trade. Original draft position has no effect on reaching the playoffs, but teams that acquire more total draft value through trading are significantly more likely to reach the playoffs 2–3 years later. Most teams use McCoy's outdated Pick Value Chart (PVC), which overvalues early picks relative to more accurate models. Teams like New England that recognized this mispricing gained systematic advantages and remained dominant for over two decades. For football front offices and performance staff, this study reinforces that roster building through strategic draft-pick trading—guided by empirically derived draft-value curves—is more predictive of playoff success than simply having an early pick.
methods: Retrospective analysis of all NFL drafts and game results for the 1998–2021 seasons (focused on 2002–2021 post-realignment to 32 teams). Primary data from pro-football-reference.com. Eight draft-value curves compared: McCoy's PVC, Massey-Thaler MT1 (1983–2008) and MT2 (2001–2008), Stuart's PFRAV, Schuckers' APVC, and Lopez's Average, Star, and Blended curves. Original and final draft currency computed for each team-year. Team strength measured via least squares ratings (scoring margin-based). Logistic regression (equation 1) with random effects clustered by team estimated probability of making the playoffs, controlling for prior team ratings (up to 4 lags), strength of schedule, new head coach, division, and first-round QB selections. Dataset: 635 team-season observations (32 teams, 2002–2021; Cleveland Browns and Houston Texans have fewer due to expansion). Robustness tests included team dummies, compensatory-pick-only models, Patriots exclusion, and win percentage as dependent variable.
findings: Original draft currency was not statistically significant in any of the 10 models across all 8 draft-value curves (Table 1), indicating the assigned draft position does not influence playoff probability. Final draft currency (after pick trades) was significant for the Star, Blended, PFRAV, APVC, Average, and Linear draft-value curves (Table 2), primarily at lags 2 and 3 years. Model fit (log pseudolikelihood) was substantially better for models using less-steep draft-value curves (PFRAV, APVC, Average, Linear) than for PVC-based models. Final draft currency is strongly predicted by original draft currency (β≈1.00, R²=0.67 for PVC) but varies more when using APVC (R²=0.29) and Average (R²=0.28), reflecting that some teams systematically gain or lose value in trades. The New England Patriots had the least original draft currency under APVC but ended up with more final draft currency than 21 of 31 other teams on average, contributing to 17 playoff appearances over 20 seasons. An average team improving its final draft currency from 2.6 to 2.9 (APVC) increases its estimated playoff probability from 33.8% to 38.9% (15% increase).
limitations: The authors cannot fully control for trades involving veteran players alongside draft picks, so final draft currency coefficients may be biased downward. Positional depth within the draft and team-specific roster needs are not modeled. The analysis focuses on a single dependent variable (playoff appearance) and is underpowered to study Super Bowl appearance or divisional round advancement due to small sample sizes. The surplus value curve's critical dependence on 1994–2008 compensation data limits its applicability post-2011 CBA. Results may not generalize beyond the 32-team, post-2002-realignment NFL structure.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football front offices should replace McCoy's PVC—still widely used in negotiations—with empirically derived, less-steep draft-value curves (APVC, Average, Linear, PFRAV) that better capture actual player value by draft position. Teams using updated valuation frameworks will systematically acquire more draft value in trades, which translates into measurably higher playoff probability 2–3 years later. General managers and football operations staff should treat draft-pick trading as a capital allocation problem: the total value of picks acquired post-trade is a stronger predictor of organizational success than pick position. Performance directors should understand that late-round picks are undervalued by the PVC, making them advantageous assets to acquire.
athleteDev: For college football programs and NFL teams with draft responsibilities, this study underscores the importance of talent identification across all draft rounds rather than over-investing in top picks. Because less-steep value curves (PFRAV, Average) correlate better with playoff success, organizations that develop late-round picks effectively gain disproportionate value. Athlete development infrastructure—including depth chart management, position flexibility training, and accelerated development pipelines for later-round selections—is a strategic competitive advantage. Programs should build systems that reduce developmental risk for mid-to-late round picks, where true market inefficiencies exist relative to conventional pick valuation.
rtp: Not directly applicable. This is a sports economics study and does not address player health, injury, or return-to-play.

citation: Young TS, Chatham LS, Carpenter RD, Yakacki CM. Does pad displacement correlate to impact severity metrics in football helmets? Proc IMechE Part P: J Sports Engineering and Technology. 2024;1-13. doi:10.1177/17543371241280709
abstract: This computational study used finite element (FE) models of three NFL-tested football helmets (2016 Riddell Speed Classic, 2016 Xenith X2E, 2017 Vicis Zero1) to investigate whether helmet pad displacement—separated into normal and tangential components—correlates with impact severity metrics (HIC, DAMAGE, HARM) across six impact locations and three speeds per NFL testing protocol. When evaluated across all locations combined, normal displacement did not correlate strongly with any severity metric (R²<0.44); however, at the side upper (SU) location, normal displacement correlated strongly with HARM, HIC, and PRLA (R²=0.79–0.80). Tangential displacement was not found to correlate with any impact severity metric regardless of location, and helmets that maximized normal displacement without bottoming out generally performed better on severity metrics.
tldr: Helmet pad normal displacement is a meaningful indicator of impact severity—but only at specific impact locations, most notably the side upper (SU) region, which carries the highest weight in the NFL's Helmet Performance Score. A helmet that maximizes normal displacement without the pad fully densifying (bottoming out) will produce lower HIC and HARM scores. Tangential displacement is largely irrelevant to severity metrics. For equipment managers and performance staff, this study provides mechanistic insight into why certain helmets outperform others in NFL testing: the geometry and deformation mechanics of the internal padding system matter more than outer shell shape. The Riddell helmet showed lowest peak normal displacements, while Vicis showed the highest HIC despite comparable displacements due to its buckling column design densifying earlier. When selecting helmets, understanding pad displacement characteristics by impact location—not just overall performance scores—may guide more informed decisions.
methods: FE models of three helmets (2016 Riddell Speed Classic, 2016 Xenith X2E, 2017 Vicis Zero1) from BioCore LLC were used in LS-DYNA simulations per NFL testing protocol. The Hybrid III (HIII) headform and neck assembly were paired with a linear impactor. Six impact locations: side (C), rear (D), side upper (SU), oblique front (OF), facemask side (FMS), facemask center oblique (FMCO) at impact speeds of 5.5, 7.4, and 9.3 m/s. MATLAB code extracted time history data for three nodes from the outer pad surface and three from the inner pad surface to calculate peak normal and tangential displacement per impact. Impact severity metrics calculated: HIC15 (Head Injury Criterion, 15 ms window), DAMAGE (Diffuse Axonal Multi-Axis General Evaluation), HARM (Head Acceleration Response Metric, weighted combination of HIC and DAMAGE). Model validation used CORA scores: Riddell 0.79, Xenith 0.79, Vicis 0.74. Correlations between peak displacement components and severity metrics assessed using R² values by location and combined.
findings: Normal displacement correlated strongly with severity metrics at the SU location (HARM R²=0.79, HIC R²=0.80, PRLA R²=0.79) but weakly at other locations (OF: HARM R²=0.06, HIC R²=0.00). Across all locations combined, R²=0.33–0.44 for normal displacement versus severity metrics. Tangential displacement showed weak correlations across all locations (R²<0.2 for all metrics except PRAA at D location, R²=0.65). Peak normal displacement increased with impact speed for most locations; maximum deformations ranged 12–37mm across helmets at highest speed. At the rear location, Vicis showed highest HIC despite comparable displacements due to premature pad densification from buckling column design. Riddell had lowest normal displacements and HIC was variable by location; Xenith showed lowest HIC at the OF location. At the SU location, all three helmets reached similar peak normal displacements but Vicis showed highest HIC (Figure 6c) due to acceleration time-history shape. The hypothesis that helmets maximizing normal displacement without bottoming out will perform better was supported at SU and OF but not uniformly at all locations.
limitations: Only three impact speeds per location were tested, limiting the ability to statistically assess correlations (three data points per helmet per location). FE models are several years old and have largely been replaced by newer helmet versions. FE model definitions for rear location may not fully represent real-world conditions due to omission of rear nape of head geometry. Tangential displacement is calculated as a resultant without directional information. HIC, DAMAGE, and HARM each have inherent limitations in capturing all aspects of concussion risk. Results depend on specific materials and geometries in the BioCore FE models and may not generalize to all helmet designs.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football equipment managers should understand that impact location is critical when interpreting helmet performance scores. A helmet that performs well at the side upper (SU) location—the most heavily weighted in NFL HPS rankings—will benefit most from maximizing normal pad displacement without bottoming out. The Vicis buckling column design, while innovative, may reach pad densification earlier than traditional foam designs, which can paradoxically increase HIC at some locations. Equipment staff should use NFL helmet testing data alongside this biomechanical framework to select helmets that balance protection across all impact zones, not only those with the highest aggregate HPS score. Design engineers should prioritize pad systems that sustain energy absorption (displacement) across a range of impact speeds before full densification.
athleteDev: For football programs selecting helmets for developmental athletes, this study indicates that helmet fit and the internal pad-to-head interface—ensuring pads make full contact and displace effectively during impact—are critical for optimal protection. Helmets with larger internal offset (like Vicis) may provide less effective energy absorption if the design leads to premature densification at moderate impact speeds typical of youth and collegiate play. Position-specific helmet selection considerations are supported by the location-dependent nature of impact severity correlations; linemen experiencing frequent frontal impacts may benefit from helmets with different pad characteristics than skill players experiencing lateral impacts.
rtp: While this study does not directly address return-to-play, it provides mechanistic context relevant to concussion prevention protocols. The finding that helmet pad normal displacement correlates with HARM scores at high-risk impact locations (SU) reinforces that proper helmet fit and pad condition are critical components of concussion risk management. Equipment staff conducting return-to-play evaluations should include helmet inspection—checking pad integrity and displacement capacity—as part of the clearance process, particularly after a concussive impact. Helmets whose pads have experienced full densification from a prior impact may have reduced protective capacity for subsequent impacts.

citation: Card RK, Harrington JM, Reyes NP, Huish EG Jr, Pettegrew J. Does Playing Surface Affect the Risk of Anterior Cruciate Ligament Injuries in the National Football League? A Look at the 2017-2021 NFL Season. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. 2023. doi:10.1080/00913847.2023.2178817
abstract: This epidemiological study examined the prevalence and incidence of ACL injuries across natural grass, synthetic turf, and GrassMaster (hybrid) surfaces in the NFL during the 2017/18 through 2021/22 seasons using publicly available data. A total of 173 ACL ruptures with identifiable playing surfaces were recorded across 1,603 games: 73 on grass, 85 on turf, and 15 on GrassMaster. ACL injury rates were 0.097 per game on grass, 0.115 on turf, and 0.134 on GrassMaster, yielding a non-natural surface injury rate of 0.118. The injury rate ratio for non-natural vs. natural grass surfaces was 1.211 (21.1% higher risk), and the odds ratio was 1.239 (95% CI 0.900–1.704, p=0.189), which did not reach statistical significance. NOTE: This file (ID 157, filename ending '...NFL season.pdf') and ID 158 (filename ending '...NFL seasons.pdf') are identical in content — same authors, DOI, abstract, results, and data tables. The only difference is the filename: singular 'season' vs. plural 'seasons'. Both files appear to be the same accepted manuscript version.
tldr: Non-natural playing surfaces (synthetic turf and GrassMaster combined) carry a 21.1% higher ACL injury rate compared to natural grass in the NFL over five seasons (2017–2021), but this difference does not reach statistical significance (OR=1.239, 95% CI 0.900–1.704, p=0.189). GrassMaster hybrid surfaces showed the highest injury rate (0.134/game), followed by turf (0.115) and grass (0.097). The study is the first to include GrassMaster in NFL surface analysis. Despite the trend being consistent with prior literature, the lack of statistical significance—likely due to limited sample size and reliance on publicly available data (which captures only ~68% of true NFL ACL injuries)—means definitive conclusions cannot be drawn. Football operations staff should note the consistent trend and advocate for collaboration with the NFL on internal injury surveillance data to produce more definitive surface safety evidence. NOTE: IDs 157 and 158 are identical papers with different filenames.
methods: Retrospective epidemiological study using publicly available data (NFL team press releases, game reports, published injury reports) for NFL preseason, regular season, and playoff games from the 2017/18 through 2021/22 seasons. ACL injuries were included if they involved confirmed rupture or tear during a game where the playing surface could be identified; practice injuries and OTA injuries were excluded. Playing surfaces classified as: natural grass (Kentucky Bluegrass, Bermuda, or blended grass), synthetic turf (100% synthetic, multiple manufacturers), or GrassMaster (Tarkett Sports hybrid surface with synthetic fibers embedded in natural grass sub-base). ACL injuries recorded by field type (73 grass, 85 turf, 15 GrassMaster). Total games: 1,603 (752 grass, 739 turf, 112 GrassMaster). Injury rate defined as ACL ruptures per game. Injury rate ratios and odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals calculated; statistical analysis performed by a senior author (EH).
findings: 173 total ACL ruptures on identifiable surfaces across 1,603 games. Grass: 73 injuries (42.1%), 752 games; Turf: 85 injuries (49.1%), 739 games; GrassMaster: 15 injuries (8.7%), 112 games. ACL injury rates per game: Grass 0.097, Turf 0.115, GrassMaster 0.134, Non-natural combined 0.118. Injury rate ratio turf vs. grass: 1.185 (18.5% increased risk on turf). Injury rate ratio non-natural vs. grass: 1.211 (21.1% increased risk). OR for turf vs. grass: 1.209 (95% CI 0.869–1.683, p=0.261). OR for non-natural vs. grass: 1.239 (95% CI 0.900–1.704, p=0.189). No statistically significant difference in ACL injury risk between natural and non-natural surfaces. The trend is consistent with prior studies showing elevated ACL injury rates on synthetic surfaces.
limitations: Reliance on publicly available data, which captures approximately 68% of true NFL ACL injuries reported to NFL medical staff (per Inclan et al., 2022), may underestimate the true incidence and potentially introduce bias. Practice injuries were excluded due to inability to identify playing surface, even though many teams use both natural and synthetic practice surfaces. Only the game surface (not practice surface) was attributable to each injury. Study is limited to preseason, regular season, and playoff games. Sample size may be insufficient to detect statistically significant differences; the authors note that collaboration with the NFL to access internal medical records would substantially strengthen future analyses. The GrassMaster surface was used by only two NFL teams (Philadelphia Eagles and Green Bay Packers), limiting its representativeness.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football medical and performance staffs should be aware that a consistent—though not statistically significant—trend of 18–21% higher ACL injury rates exists on non-natural playing surfaces compared to grass in the NFL. GrassMaster hybrid surfaces showed the highest per-game injury rate in this dataset. Teams playing home games on synthetic surfaces should consider this trend when designing ACL prevention programs and load management strategies around surface type. Advocacy for NFL-level access to internal injury surveillance data is needed to generate statistically powered studies. Until more definitive data is available, surface type should be considered one factor among many in individualized ACL risk profiling.
athleteDev: Youth through college football programs should consider playing surface as a contextual risk factor in ACL injury prevention programming. Athletes regularly training or competing on synthetic turf may benefit from surface-specific neuromuscular training that addresses the higher traction and altered lower extremity mechanics associated with artificial surfaces. Footwear optimization (cleat type and length matched to surface) is a modifiable variable that may reduce traction-related ACL injury risk. Programs with access to both surface types should consider assigning highest-intensity ACL-risk activities (cutting drills, reactive agility) to natural grass when feasible.
rtp: Surface type should be considered in return-to-play planning for NFL players recovering from ACL reconstruction. Given the trend toward higher ACL injury incidence on synthetic turf and GrassMaster, a conservative approach to first full-contact return on these surfaces is warranted. Progressive surface reintroduction—beginning with natural grass and advancing to synthetic turf—may be appropriate for athletes with a history of ACL injury, particularly when returning to competition at venues with non-natural surfaces. The approximately 10.75-month average RTP timeline for NFL ACL injuries should factor in surface type and the athlete's neuromuscular readiness for cutting and planting mechanics on high-traction artificial turf.
